title: Structured Strength Training & Energy Systems
pillar_name: Physical Literacy, Martial Arts & Human Performance

required.title: [Equipment] Barbell, squat rack, and weight plates

competence_markers.description: The student can perform squat, deadlift, overhead press, bench press, and barbell row with correct form under load
The student can deadlift bodyweight for reps (multiple reps at bodyweight on the bar)
The student can perform 5+ strict pull-ups
The student can run 1 mile in under 8 minutes (boys) / 9 minutes (girls)
The student can carry 50% bodyweight for 400 meters
The student can ruck 4+ miles with 20–25% bodyweight
The student can explain the three energy systems and identify which system is dominant in a given activity
The student can sustain 30 minutes of continuous physical work without significant performance degradation
The student maintains a training log with visible progression over weeks
The student trains consistently (3x/week lifting, 2–3x/week conditioning) without requiring external motivation

cross_connections.description: Mobility and movement quality make barbell training safe. A student who cannot squat to depth with bodyweight is not ready for a loaded squat.
Strength and conditioning directly enhance martial arts performance. Grip strength for grappling. Hip power for takedowns. Conditioning for sustained rounds.
Strength training without adequate protein and sleep produces minimal results. The connection is direct and unavoidable.
Following a training program on the days you do not feel like training is IC2 at its most concrete.

content.why_this_matters:
You are now old enough and physically prepared to train with the most effective strength-building tool ever created: the barbell. A barbell loaded with weight plates, used with proper technique and progressive overload, builds strength faster and more efficiently than any other method. Every military, every serious strength sport, and every physical preparation program in the world uses barbell training as its foundation for a reason — it works.

But the barbell is also unforgiving. It does not care about your ego, your impatience, or your desire to load more weight than you can handle. A barbell lifted with bad technique punishes you — not today, but over hundreds of repetitions, as bad mechanics grind your joints into dysfunction. This packet teaches you to lift correctly first, then lift heavy. That sequence is not negotiable.

You will also learn how your body produces energy — the three energy systems that power everything from a one-second explosive effort to a 10-mile ruck march. Understanding these systems lets you train intelligently rather than randomly. You will know why sprints feel different from distance runs, why a heavy set of 3 depletes you differently than a set of 20, and how to program training that develops the specific physical quality you want.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Barbell competence** — squat, deadlift, overhead press, bench press, and barbell row with correct form
2. **Kettlebell proficiency** — swing, goblet squat, Turkish get-up, clean, and press
3. **Energy systems understanding** — phosphocreatine, glycolytic, and oxidative systems explained and applied
4. **Structured conditioning** — interval training, longer aerobic work, rucking with progressive load
5. **Progressive overload programming** — adding weight and volume systematically over weeks
6. **Work capacity development** — the ability to sustain physical output for extended periods
7. **Training standards** — specific benchmarks for age-appropriate strength and conditioning
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Barbell — Why It Is the Best Tool

A barbell allows you to load any movement from very light (just the bar — 45 pounds for a standard Olympic bar, or 15–35 pounds for a training bar) to very heavy (hundreds of pounds). It allows incremental loading — you can add 2.5 pounds at a time, which means you can progress in small, manageable steps. And it trains compound movements — movements that use multiple joints and muscle groups simultaneously — which are more efficient and more transferable to real-world capability than any isolation exercise.

**The five fundamental barbell lifts:**

**Squat:** The bar rests on your upper back. You squat until your hips are below your knees. You stand up. The squat trains your legs, hips, core, and back simultaneously. It is the single most important barbell exercise.

**Deadlift:** The bar starts on the floor. You grip it, brace your core, and stand up. The deadlift trains your entire posterior chain — hamstrings, glutes, back, grip — in the most fundamental human movement pattern: picking something heavy off the ground.

**Overhead press:** The bar starts at shoulder height. You press it overhead until your arms are locked out. This trains shoulders, triceps, and core stability. It is the oldest barbell lift and the most honest — there is no cheating an overhead press.

**Bench press:** Lying on a bench, you lower the bar to your chest and press it back up. Trains chest, shoulders, and triceps. The most popular gym lift, though arguably less functional than the overhead press.

**Barbell row:** Bent over at the hips, you pull the bar from arm's length to your lower chest. Trains your upper back, lats, and biceps. Essential for balanced development and posture.

### Part 2: Learning the Lifts

**Form first. Load later. Always.**

You begin every lift with an empty bar or PVC pipe. You learn the positions, the bracing, the bar path, the breathing. You practice until your mentor or coach says your form is solid. Then — and only then — you add weight. In small increments. 5 pounds at a time. Every session, a little more.

**The squat in detail:**
1. Bar position: resting on the meat of your upper traps (high bar) or on the rear deltoids (low bar). Not on your neck bones.
2. Stance: feet shoulder-width or slightly wider, toes pointed out 20–30 degrees.
3. Brace: big breath into your belly, tighten your core, create intra-abdominal pressure.
4. Descent: break at the hips and knees simultaneously. Push your knees out over your toes. Keep your chest up. Go until your hip crease is below the top of your knee — that is depth. Anything less is a half-squat.
5. Ascent: drive up from the bottom. Do not let your chest drop or your knees cave. Push the floor away with your whole foot.

**The deadlift in detail:**
1. Bar position: over the middle of your foot, approximately 1 inch from your shins.
2. Grip: hands just outside your knees. Double overhand grip to start (switch to mixed grip at heavier weights).
3. Setup: bend at the hips and knees, grab the bar, flatten your back, squeeze your chest up.
4. Pull: push the floor away with your legs while keeping the bar against your body. The bar travels straight up. Your back angle stays constant until the bar passes your knees, then your hips drive forward to lockout.
5. Lower: reverse the movement. Controlled descent. Do not drop the bar.

### Part 3: The Three Energy Systems

Your body produces energy in three ways, and which system is dominant depends on the intensity and duration of the effort.

**Phosphocreatine system (0–10 seconds):** Explosive, maximum-effort work. A one-rep max deadlift. A 40-yard sprint. This system provides enormous power for a very short time, then it is depleted and needs 2–5 minutes to fully recover. This is why powerlifters rest 3–5 minutes between heavy sets.

**Glycolytic system (30 seconds – 2 minutes):** High-intensity sustained work. A set of 10 heavy squats. A 400-meter sprint. A hard round of wrestling. This system burns glucose (sugar) and produces lactic acid as a byproduct — the burning sensation in your muscles during hard effort. Recovery takes 1–3 minutes between efforts.

**Oxidative system (2+ minutes, up to hours):** Moderate-intensity sustained work. A 5-mile run. A long ruck march. A full day of manual labor. This system uses oxygen to burn fuel (fat and carbohydrates) and can sustain effort almost indefinitely at moderate intensities. It recovers between sessions, not between sets.

**Why this matters:** If you want to be strong, train the phosphocreatine system (heavy weights, low reps, long rest). If you want muscle endurance, train the glycolytic system (moderate weights, moderate reps, shorter rest). If you want cardiovascular endurance, train the oxidative system (sustained moderate effort). Most people need all three.

### Part 4: Conditioning Protocols

**Sprint intervals:** 8–10 sprints of 30–60 yards with walk-back rest. Or: 6–8 sprints of 200 meters with 90-second rest. This trains the glycolytic system — the system most relevant to sport and martial arts performance.

**EMOM (Every Minute On the Minute):** Set a timer. At the start of every minute, perform a prescribed exercise (10 kettlebell swings, 5 push-ups, 3 pull-ups). Rest the remainder of the minute. Continue for 10–20 minutes. This builds work capacity under time pressure.

**Rucking:** Progressive ruck marches. Start at 2 miles with 20% bodyweight. Build toward 6+ miles with 25–30% bodyweight. This is the single best conditioning method for building leg strength, cardiovascular fitness, and mental toughness simultaneously.

**Longer aerobic work:** 2–5 mile runs at conversation pace. The goal is building an aerobic base — the ability to sustain moderate effort for 30+ minutes. This supports recovery from hard training and builds the cardiovascular foundation for everything else.

### Part 5: Programming Your Training

A simple beginner program (3 days per week):

**Day A:**
- Squat: 3 sets of 5
- Overhead Press: 3 sets of 5
- Deadlift: 1 set of 5

**Day B:**
- Squat: 3 sets of 5
- Bench Press: 3 sets of 5
- Barbell Row: 3 sets of 5

Alternate Day A and Day B. Add 5 pounds to each lift every session (2.5 lbs per side). When you stall on a lift (cannot complete all sets), reduce the weight by 10% and work back up. This is the novice linear progression and it works for months.

**Conditioning (2–3 additional days per week):** One sprint session, one ruck or longer run, one kettlebell session. Do not condition on the same day you lift heavy if possible — or if you must, lift first and condition after.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Barbell Technique
**Developing well:** All five lifts performed with consistent form. The student self-corrects when they feel form breaking. They can describe the key technical points of each lift. Film review shows clean movement patterns.

**Developing slowly:** Persistent form errors despite coaching. Cannot self-correct. Loads weight despite form degradation.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student had enough empty-bar practice? Most technique problems come from rushing past the learning phase. Go back to the empty bar and rebuild if needed.

### 2. Strength Progression
**Developing well:** Adding weight consistently over weeks. Training log shows clear linear progression. Stalls are managed appropriately (reduce weight, rebuild).

**Developing slowly:** No progression for weeks. Training log is empty or inconsistent. Skipping sessions.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student eating and sleeping enough? Novice lifters who stall early are almost always under-eating, under-sleeping, or missing sessions. Check all three before blaming programming.

### 3. Energy Systems Understanding
**Developing well:** Can explain all three systems and correctly identify which system a given activity primarily uses. Applies this understanding to their own training — understands why rest periods matter, why sprints and distance runs develop different qualities.

**Developing slowly:** Can name the systems but cannot apply the knowledge.

### 4. Conditioning
**Developing well:** Meets or approaches the benchmarks (sub-8-minute mile, 50% BW carry for 400m, 4+ mile ruck). Training includes both sprint and endurance work.

**Developing slowly:** Conditioning is neglected in favor of lifting. Or lifting is neglected in favor of conditioning. The student should be doing both.
